reports.title: DeepSeek Builds the World's Best Open-Source AI on Chinese Chips. The US Just Made It Official.
reports.subtitle: Washington cleared Nvidia H200 exports to China four months ago. China blocked its own companies from buying them. DeepSeek then launched V4 on Huawei hardware and undercut every American model on price.

reports.title: Congress Wants to Force US Allies to Choose: Sell Chip Equipment to China, or Sell to America
reports.subtitle: The MATCH Act passed committee and gives the Netherlands and Japan 150 days to align their export restrictions with US rules or face their own restrictions. The allies have not agreed.

reports.title: The EPA Just Deleted the Legal Foundation for Every US Climate Rule. 25 States Are Suing.
reports.subtitle: The 2009 Endangerment Finding said greenhouse gases endanger public health. Every US climate regulation for 17 years was built on it. The Trump EPA repealed it last week.

reports.title: One Year After India Struck Pakistan, Both Countries Are More Dangerous Than Before
reports.subtitle: Operation Sindoor destroyed nine terror camps and suspended a 60-year water treaty. India's military meets this week to plan what comes next. Pakistan is at the UN Security Council, running out of water.

reports.title: Harvard's Graduate Workers Are on Strike While Trump Freezes $2.2 Billion in Grants
reports.subtitle: 4,000 workers walked out demanding higher wages and visa protections for international students. Harvard's administration is simultaneously fighting a federal funding freeze. Everyone is using everyone else as leverage.

reports.title: Google Buys a Rival It Cannot Control
reports.subtitle: A $40 billion bet on Anthropic is less an investment than a structural admission that Google lost the AI race internally.

reports.title: The Justice Department Dismantles DACA From the Inside
reports.subtitle: By ruling that DACA status alone cannot block deportation, the administration's own immigration courts have accomplished what congressional repeal never could.

reports.title: Iran Ceasefire Talks Break Down as War Powers Clock Ticks
reports.subtitle: With Trump's envoys a no-show in Islamabad and Tehran's diplomat already on a plane to Oman, the US faces a constitutional deadline in five days that neither side has acknowledged publicly.

reports.title: $166 Billion Goes Back to Walmart. American Families Get Nothing.
reports.subtitle: The Supreme Court ruled Trump's tariffs illegal, the government is now refunding the money, and the legal structure guarantees that corporations receive every dollar while the consumers who bore the costs are locked out entirely.

reports.title: The World Is Copying Australia's Teen Social Media Ban. Australia's Is Already Failing.
reports.subtitle: Four months after Australia became the first country to ban social media for under-16s, 61% of affected teens are still on the platforms, and Turkey, Norway, Manitoba, and a dozen US states are lining up to enact the same policy.

reports.title: Trump Escapes the White House Correspondents' Dinner. Now He Wants to Escape the Venue Entirely.
reports.subtitle: A gunman breached security at the annual press dinner, and before the night was over, Trump was using the chaos to accelerate a plan his lawyers had been blocking for months.

reports.title: The DOJ Restores the Firing Squad. The Pope Disagrees.
reports.subtitle: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche reinstated federal executions by firing squad the same week the newly elected Pope Leo called for abolishing the death penalty worldwide.

reports.title: Zelensky Picks Baku. Russia Does Not Need to Accept.
reports.subtitle: Ukraine's president made his first wartime visit to Azerbaijan, signed six defense and energy agreements, and proposed it as the venue for peace talks with Russia, knowing Putin will almost certainly say no.

reports.title: Trump Is Suing an Agency He Controls. A Judge Wants to Know How That Works.
reports.subtitle: A federal judge has paused Trump's $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS, noting that a president cannot be 'sufficiently adverse' to his own executive branch to satisfy the Constitution's requirements for a real legal dispute.

reports.title: RFK Jr. Told the Senate He Is Not Cutting Medicaid. His Budget Cuts $15.8 Billion From HHS.
reports.subtitle: While RFK Jr. testified that he was not reducing Medicaid coverage, his own 2027 budget proposal would impose new eligibility restrictions, eliminate payments to family caregivers, and defund programs used disproportionately by disabled children and rural hospitals.

reports.title: The Trial That Could Unwind a Trillion-Dollar Company
reports.subtitle: Jury selection begins in Musk v. Altman, a case where the most damaging evidence is OpenAI's own co-founder calling his nonprofit commitment 'a lie.'

reports.title: The Fed Gets a New Chair Who Promised 'Regime Change'
reports.subtitle: Kevin Warsh's path to the Federal Reserve clears after the DOJ drops its Powell probe, but his plan to cut rates collides directly with an oil shock he never accounted for.

reports.title: The EU Sanctioned China to Hurt Russia. Now China Is Sanctioning the EU's Defense Industry.
reports.subtitle: Brussels put 27 Chinese firms on its Russia blacklist; Beijing responded by targeting European defense companies and warning the EU will 'bear all consequences' of not reversing course.

reports.title: The Supreme Court Looks Poised to Rule Against Trump. His Own Party Is Already Moving Past It.
reports.subtitle: With 64% of Americans opposing birthright citizenship repeal and the conservative-majority court appearing skeptical, Senate Republicans are advancing legislation that would accomplish the same result differently.

reports.title: Trump Signed a Mail Voting Order. 23 States Sued Within Hours. The Midterms Are in 6 Months.
reports.subtitle: An executive order requiring USPS to verify voter citizenship before delivering ballots is legally dubious, operationally impossible, and may energize the very voters it aims to suppress.
